sections.Background and Epidemiology: Acute right ventricular failure (RVF) accounts for 2–9% of acute heart failure admissions with in-hospital mortality of 5–17%. Definitions (Harjola et al. 2016): Right heart failure (RHF) = clinical syndrome reducing pulmonary blood flow ± high systemic venous pressures. Acute RV dysfunction (RVD) = acute RV systolic dysfunction/dilatation on TTE, OR raised natriuretic peptides without LV/renal impairment, OR RV strain on ECG. Acute RVF = RVD plus low cardiac output and hypoperfusion causing multi-organ dysfunction.
sections.The RV in Health and Disease: RV: crescent-shaped, 2 fibre layers, free wall 2–5mm (LV: ellipsoidal, 3 layers, 8–10mm). RV stroke work ~1/4 of LV despite equal stroke volume; coupled to a low-resistance, high-compliance pulmonary circulation (PVR ~1/5 of SVR). RV PV loop is triangular (shortened/absent isovolumetric relaxation) vs LV's rectangular loop. RV is 'tolerant' of preload, 'intolerant' of afterload — stroke volume falls steeply with acute afterload rises (e.g. PE) but adapts to chronic pressure overload (e.g. PH) via hypertrophy. Ventricular interdependence: RV volume/pressure overload shifts the septum toward the LV ('D-shaped' LV), impairing LV filling. PVR is lowest at FRC (alveolar vessel resistance rises with lung inflation; extra-alveolar vessel resistance falls). Downward spiral of RVF: pressure overload → reduced CO → systemic hypotension → reduced RV coronary perfusion → RV ischaemia → further reduced contractility.
sections.RV Dysfunction in the ICU: RV dysfunction occurs in 22–55% of ARDS (independently associated with mortality); predictors include lower respiratory tract infection cause, driving pressure ≥18cmH2O, PaO2/FiO2 <150mmHg, PaCO2 ≥48mmHg. Cor pulmonale in ARDS is associated with significantly worse 28-day survival. Massive PE causes cardiogenic shock in 4.5% of cases; RVD on TTE present in 30-56% of confirmed PE; 90-day mortality ~52% in massive PE. PH (mPAP≥25mmHg): WHO Group I (PAH) vs Group II (left heart disease, most common cause overall) vs Group III (lung disease/hypoxia) vs Group IV (CTEPH, 2-4% post-PE) vs Group V (multifactorial). COPD is the commonest cause of cor pulmonale; RV changes occur even in mild disease. Cardiac surgery causes RVF in up to 30% (air embolism to anteriorly-placed right coronary ostia, myocardial stunning, post-CPB PVR rise, TR repair unmasking RV impairment). LVAD implantation increases RV preload demand; RV Failure Risk Score (vasopressors 4pts, AST>80 2pts, bilirubin>2.0 2.5pts, creatinine>2.3 3pts; >5.5 = high risk) predicts post-LVAD RVF.
sections.Clinical Assessment, Workup and Monitori: High suspicion required — classic signs often absent in ventilated/sedated patients. Biomarkers (BNP, troponin) non-specific but useful for monitoring. ECG often normal; S1Q3T3 pattern (RV strain) present in <10% of PE. CXR: RV enlargement seen on lateral view (>50% of retrosternal space); clear lung fields with low CO suggest RVF. CVP/JVP: raised JVP = specific for right-sided failure; Kussmaul's sign = paradoxical JVP rise on inspiration (RV infarction, constrictive pericarditis); severe TR produces fused c-v waves with sharp y descent; reduced RV compliance produces accentuated a wave with attenuated y wave. PAC: gold standard for PH diagnosis; RVD suggested by CVP>PCWP, low CI/SVI, SvO2<55%. TTE first-line (ASE 2010 guidelines); D-shaped LV on parasternal short-axis view reflects RV pressure/volume overload via ventricular interdependence. TOE: Category I indication in unstable patients with unexplained haemodynamics when TTE inconclusive; oesophageal perforation risk ~1/500, mortality ~1/10,000.
sections.Management: Physiological Targets: Systematic approach: rate/rhythm, coronary perfusion, preload, contractility, afterload. Rate/rhythm: higher HR (~90-110bpm) and sinus rhythm preferred to limit RV distension and maintain CO. Coronary perfusion: maintaining systemic (and hence right coronary) pressure is critical and often under-appreciated — in a canine PA-banding model, restoring MAP/RCA pressure with phenylephrine (without changing mechanical obstruction) reduced RVEDP and increased CO. Preload: small fluid boluses (50-100ml) with close CVP monitoring; RVMI (normal afterload) may need higher RVEDP; elevated-afterload RVF (e.g. PAH) needs diuresis, not fluid loading. Contractility: inodilators — dobutamine (β1-agonist) and milrinone (PDE-3 inhibitor, longer half-life ~2.5h) — reduce SVR/PVR while increasing contractility; risk of systemic hypotension requires concurrent vasoconstrictors (noradrenaline, vasopressin — the latter causing pulmonary vasodilation at low doses via endothelial NO). Afterload: avoid hypoxia/hypercapnia/acidosis/atelectasis/excessive PEEP; IV pulmonary vasodilators cause indiscriminate vasodilation and V/Q mismatch worsening; inhaled NO reduces PVR selectively in ventilated lung regions improving V/Q matching; sildenafil (PDE-5 inhibitor, long half-life 4-18h) used in stable chronic disease.
sections.Short-term Mechanical Circulatory Suppor: Considered in RV failure refractory to medical therapy; exit strategy should be defined early. Impella RP: transvalvular axial-flow, RA→PA, up to 4L/min, ↓RA pressure/↑PA systolic pressure/↓native RV SV, CE-marked to 14 days, ACT target 160-180s. TandemHeart RVAD: centrifugal, RA→PA (dual cannula or PROTEK Duo), up to 4L/min, similar haemodynamics to Impella RP, ACT target 180-220s. VA-ECMO: centrifugal, RA→systemic artery, >5L/min, ↓RA pressure but NO effect on PA systolic pressure or native RV SV (bypasses rather than unloads the pulmonary circulation); biventricular + respiratory support; AR and severe PVD are contraindications; typically <7 days, up to 21+ in some cases. Surgical RVAD (e.g. CentriMag): higher flows (>5L/min), central cannulation allows mobilisation, CE-marked to 30 days.
sections.Long-term MCS and Transplantation: Long-term LVADs used right-sided remain off-label/controversial (small case series, significant complications). Total Artificial Heart (TAH): pneumatic biventricular support, bridge to transplant, useful when ventricular cavities too small for LVAD (e.g. restrictive/hypertrophic cardiomyopathy) or predominantly right-sided disease. Heart transplant: indicated for refractory advanced HF without significant comorbidity; contraindicated in end-stage RHF from irreversible PH (donor RV cannot tolerate recipient's elevated PVR). Primary graft dysfunction-RV (PGD-RV): high right-sided filling + LOW left-sided filling + LOW CO with low pulmonary pressures, OR need for RV MCS within 24h of surgery. Lung transplant alone (without heart) can allow RV recovery when RVF is driven by lung disease; post-transplant LV failure can occur as a chronically underfilled LV is suddenly exposed to normalised CO/preload once PVR falls.

questions.question: A mechanically ventilated patient with severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 115, PEEP 14 cmH2O) develops progressive hypotension. Bedside TTE shows a dilated RV with D-shaped interventricular septum and septal dyskinesia. Pulse pressure variation (PPV) is 19%. What does this finding most likely indicate?
options: A: Severe LV systolic dysfunction as the primary haemodynamic problem
B: RV afterload dependency — PPV reflects failing RV output, not volume responsiveness
C: Hypovolaemia requiring a 500 ml fluid bolus to restore cardiac output
D: Cardiac tamponade requiring urgent pericardiocentesis
E: Excessive PEEP compressing the RV and requiring immediate PEEP reduction
questions.explanation: In patients with RV dysfunction on positive pressure ventilation, elevated PPV reflects beat-to-beat variation due to RV afterload dependency, NOT fluid responsiveness. Administering fluids misled by this finding can severely worsen RV dilatation, tricuspid regurgitation and ventricular interdependence — a recognised and dangerous clinical pitfall. The D-shaped septum with septal dyskinesia confirms cor pulmonale. Reducing PEEP in this context may worsen hypoxia and further increase PVR via hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, rather than address the underlying RV afterload dependency.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RVF ARDS pulse-pressure-variation heart-lung-interaction ventricular-interdependence

questions.question: A 61-year-old woman is brought to the ED in cardiogenic shock. BP is 72/44 mmHg and has been persistently hypotensive for 25 minutes without another identifiable cause. ECG shows S1Q3T3 pattern with right bundle branch block. Bedside TTE demonstrates a dilated, hypokinetic RV with paradoxical septal motion. CT-PA confirms massive bilateral PE. She has no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Systemic thrombolysis
B: Catheter-directed thrombolysis via interventional radiology
C: Emergency surgical embolectomy
D: Therapeutic anticoagulation with LMWH and close monitoring in the ICU
E: Milrinone infusion to improve RV contractility while awaiting imaging review
questions.explanation: This is a high-risk (massive) PE defined by sustained haemodynamic compromise (BP drop >40 mmHg for >15 min without another cause). With no contraindications, systemic thrombolysis is the primary reperfusion strategy. Catheter-directed thrombolysis and surgical embolectomy are reserved for patients with contraindications to systemic thrombolysis. Anticoagulation with LMWH alone is insufficient reperfusion in a shocked patient with massive PE. An inodilator such as milrinone cannot address the obstructive pathophysiology and risks worsening systemic hypotension.
questions.topic: massive-PE thrombolysis reperfusion high-risk cardiogenic-shock RVF

questions.question: A patient with known idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group I) is admitted with acute RVF and systemic hypotension (MAP 46 mmHg). Vasopressor support is initiated. Which agent has the advantage of producing pulmonary vasodilatation at low doses via the endothelial nitric oxide pathway, while simultaneously maintaining systemic pressure?
options: A: Noradrenaline
B: Adrenaline
C: Vasopressin
D: Phenylephrine
E: Metaraminol
questions.explanation: At low doses (0.01–0.04 units/min), vasopressin causes systemic vasoconstriction via V1 receptors while producing pulmonary vasodilatation through the endothelial nitric oxide pathway — a pharmacologically advantageous profile in PAH where minimising further PVR increases is critical. Noradrenaline is commonly used and increases PVR at higher doses. Phenylephrine and metaraminol are pure α1-agonists without selective pulmonary vasodilating properties. Adrenaline causes tachycardia and increases myocardial oxygen demand, both detrimental in acute RVF.
questions.topic: vasopressin PAH pulmonary-vasodilatation nitric-oxide vasopressor RVF

questions.question: A 54-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy is assessed for LVAD implantation. Pre-operative values: requiring vasopressor support (yes), AST 95 IU/L, bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, creatinine 2.5 mg/dL. What is his RV Failure Risk Score and what does it predict?
options: A: Score 4; low risk of post-LVAD RVF (vasopressors only counted)
B: Score 9; high risk of post-LVAD RVF
C: Score 5.5; borderline risk of post-LVAD RVF
D: Score 6; high risk of post-LVAD RVF
E: Score 11.5; high risk of post-LVAD RVF
questions.explanation: The RV Failure Risk Score assigns: vasopressors (4 pts) + AST >80 IU/L (2 pts) + bilirubin >2.0 mg/dL (0 pts — bilirubin is 1.8, below threshold) + creatinine >2.3 mg/dL (3 pts) = 9 points. A score >5.5 predicts high risk of RVF after LVAD insertion. The 11.5-point distractor is the near-miss: it incorrectly awards points for bilirubin, which at 1.8 mg/dL falls below the >2.0 mg/dL threshold and should score zero. This scoring system outperforms PVR, PA systolic pressure and RA pressure as a predictor of post-LVAD RVF.
questions.topic: LVAD RVF-risk-score vasopressors bilirubin creatinine AST post-operative

questions.question: Which of the following statements about right ventricular physiology is INCORRECT?
options: A: Ejection of blood from the RV can continue despite falling ventricular pressure due to momentum in the low-pressure pulmonary system
B: The RV pressure-volume loop appears more triangular than the LV loop due to a shortened or absent isovolumetric relaxation phase
C: RV stroke work is approximately one-quarter of LV stroke work despite equivalent stroke volumes
D: Normal RV systolic pressure is 90–140 mmHg, similar to mean arterial pressure
E: RV free wall thickness is 2–5 mm compared to 8–10 mm for the LV
questions.explanation: Normal RV systolic pressure is 15–30 mmHg, approximately one-fifth of systemic arterial pressure (90–140 mmHg). Stating that RV systolic pressure equals systemic pressure is incorrect and would represent end-stage severe pulmonary hypertension, not normal physiology. The remaining statements are all correct: the triangular PV loop, continued ejection despite falling pressure, lower stroke work, and thin wall are all genuine hallmarks of normal RV physiology.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RV-physiology pressure-volume-loop systolic-pressure stroke-work anatomy

questions.question: A patient with post-cardiac surgery RVF requires inotropic support. The team is considering milrinone. Which of the following best describes milrinone's mechanism of action and its most clinically significant limitation in the acute setting?
options: A: α1-agonist that increases SVR; risk of further increasing PVR at higher doses
B: Calcium sensitizer that improves contractility without increasing myocardial oxygen demand; effects can persist for up to one week
C: Phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor increasing contractility and reducing SVR/PVR; primary risk is profound systemic hypotension
D: β1-agonist that increases contractility; primary risk is tachyarrhythmias
E: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor that reduces PVR; limited by a long terminal half-life of 4–18 hours
questions.explanation: Milrinone is a PDE-3 inhibitor (inodilator) that increases contractility while simultaneously reducing SVR and PVR — an attractive profile for RVF. Its critical limitation is profound systemic hypotension, particularly in haemodynamically unstable patients, requiring concurrent systemic vasoconstrictors. Levosimendan, not milrinone, is the calcium sensitizer, with effects lasting up to one week. Sildenafil is the PDE-5 inhibitor, with a 4–18 hour half-life, used in stable disease. Dobutamine, not milrinone, is the β1-agonist.
questions.topic: milrinone PDE3-inhibitor inodilator systemic-hypotension dobutamine levosimendan RVF

questions.question: A patient with severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 90 mmHg) has echocardiographic evidence of a dilated RV with septal dyskinesia indicating cor pulmonale. The team adopts RV-protective ventilation. Which plateau pressure and driving pressure targets are supported by expert opinion for this scenario?
options: A: Plateau pressure <25 cmH2O and driving pressure <15 cmH2O
B: Plateau pressure <27 cmH2O and driving pressure <17 cmH2O
C: Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O and driving pressure <20 cmH2O (standard ARDS Network targets)
D: Tidal volume 6 ml/kg IBW with PEEP titrated to best oxygenation, regardless of plateau pressure
E: Plateau pressure <35 cmH2O and driving pressure <22 cmH2O
questions.explanation: Expert opinion (Paternot et al. 2016; Vieillard-Baron and Matthay 2013) advocates plateau pressure <27 cmH2O and driving pressure <17 cmH2O in ARDS patients with RV dysfunction — more restrictive than standard ARDS Network targets (plateau <30 cmH2O). These lower thresholds reduce transpulmonary pressure, limiting RV afterload from alveolar vessel compression. Standard ARDS Network targets are appropriate without RV dysfunction but may precipitate or worsen cor pulmonale in patients who have it. Achieving these thresholds may require accepting higher PaCO2 (permissive hypercapnia) despite its PVR-raising effects.
questions.topic: ARDS RV-protective-ventilation driving-pressure plateau-pressure cor-pulmonale

questions.question: A patient with known RVF is mechanically ventilated. Which of the following does NOT cause an acute increase in pulmonary vascular resistance?
options: A: Hypoxia (PaO2 < 8 kPa)
B: Maintaining lung volumes at FRC
C: Pulmonary atelectasis
D: Hypercapnia (PaCO2 > 6.5 kPa)
E: Metabolic acidaemia (pH 7.20)
questions.explanation: PVR is lowest at functional residual capacity (FRC) because this represents the optimal equilibrium between alveolar vessel compression (which increases with over-inflation) and extra-alveolar vessel traction, which is lost with atelectasis. Hypoxia causes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, hypercapnia and acidaemia are direct pulmonary vasoconstrictors, and atelectasis collapses extra-alveolar vessels, increasing rather than decreasing PVR. Targeting FRC minimises PVR. The atelectasis distractor is the near-miss — atelectasis increasing PVR through extra-alveolar vessel collapse is a counterintuitive but well-recognised effect.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: PVR pulmonary-vascular-resistance FRC hypoxia hypercapnia atelectasis iatrogenic

questions.question: A patient with decompensated pulmonary arterial hypertension and RVF requires pulmonary vasodilator therapy. The team chooses inhaled nitric oxide over intravenous epoprostenol. What is the key physiological advantage of inhaled nitric oxide in this context?
options: A: Direct positive inotropic effect on RV cardiomyocytes in addition to pulmonary vasodilation
B: Lower cost and simpler administration compared to IV prostacyclins
C: Availability for long-term oral administration in stable outpatients
D: Longer duration of action, allowing less frequent administration
E: Selective reduction of PVR only in ventilated lung regions, improving V/Q matching and oxygenation
questions.explanation: Inhaled nitric oxide reaches only ventilated alveoli, reducing PVR selectively in ventilated regions and improving V/Q matching by increasing blood flow to these areas. IV pulmonary vasodilators (e.g. epoprostenol, GTN) cause indiscriminate vasodilatation throughout the pulmonary vascular bed, blunting hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and worsening V/Q mismatch — and cause systemic hypotension in parallel. NO has a fast onset and short half-life. Sildenafil (not NO) has an oral form but is used in stable chronic disease due to long half-life (4–18 h) and risk of systemic hypotension.
questions.topic: inhaled-NO nitric-oxide pulmonary-vasodilator V/Q-mismatch epoprostenol RVF PAH

questions.question: A 69-year-old man with moderate COPD (FEV1 60% predicted, no prior cardiac history) is intubated for community-acquired pneumonia. Within minutes of initiation of positive pressure ventilation, he develops refractory hypotension. TTE shows a dilated RV with D-shaped septum. Which explanation is MOST consistent with this clinical picture?
options: A: PPV unmasking subclinical RV dysfunction from COPD-related pulmonary vascular remodelling
B: Anaphylaxis to the induction agent causing vasoplegia and apparent RV dilation
C: Tension pneumothorax from barotrauma during positive pressure ventilation
D: Acute PE occurring coincidentally at the time of intubation
E: Severe occult LV systolic dysfunction from ischaemic cardiomyopathy
questions.explanation: Even mild to moderate COPD is associated with subclinical RV dysfunction due to pulmonary vascular remodelling — studies show remodelling occurs even in mild disease and in smokers with normal spirometry. Positive pressure ventilation increases RA pressure (reducing RV preload) and increases RV afterload. This combination can precipitate overt RVF in an already compromised RV — the classic scenario of 'disproportionate haemodynamic collapse following intubation in COPD with single-organ respiratory failure'. The D-shaped septum confirms pressure-overloaded RV. Tension pneumothorax remains on the differential in any post-intubation deterioration but is a non-specific cause, less directly explained by this patient's COPD than the RV mechanism described above.
questions.topic: COPD cor-pulmonale intubation IPPV subclinical-RVD haemodynamic-collapse

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT contribute to acute RV failure following cardiac surgery?
options: A: Systemic vasodilation post-CPB, which reduces RV afterload
B: Myocardial stunning from direct ischaemia during cardiopulmonary bypass
C: Tricuspid valve repair unmasking latent RV impairment previously concealed by tricuspid regurgitation
D: Air embolisation to the right coronary artery due to the anterior position of the right coronary ostia
E: Post-operative rise in pulmonary vascular resistance from inflammatory mediators and protamine
questions.explanation: Systemic vasodilation commonly occurs post-CPB and actually REDUCES RV afterload — it does not cause RVF. Recognised contributors instead include: air embolisation to the anteriorly positioned right coronary ostia causing RV ischaemia; myocardial stunning from CPB; post-operative PVR rise from inflammatory mediators, extravascular lung water, de-recruitment and protamine; and TR repair, which increases effective afterload and unmasks impaired RV function previously 'flattered' by the ease of retrograde emptying. The protamine-related PVR rise is the near-miss most likely to be mistaken for the answer, since it is a well-documented but genuine contributor rather than the exception.
questions.topic: cardiac-surgery RVF air-embolism PVR protamine CPB tricuspid

questions.question: A 57-year-old woman presents with a 9-month history of progressive dyspnoea and signs of RVF. Right heart catheterisation shows mPAP 48 mmHg with normal PCWP. CT-PA demonstrates organised, fibrotic thrombus in bilateral proximal pulmonary arteries. She is anticoagulated but remains symptomatic. What is the most appropriate treatment with potentially curative intent?
options: A: Indefinite riociguat (soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator) therapy
B: Assessment for bilateral lung transplantation
C: Pulmonary endarterectomy
D: Permanent IV epoprostenol via tunnelled central line
E: Long-term anticoagulation with a DOAC and serial right heart catheterisation
questions.explanation: This patient has chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) — a complication of PE in 2–4% of patients — presenting with proximal disease that is accessible surgically. Pulmonary endarterectomy is the treatment of choice and is potentially curative in proximal CTEPH. Riociguat has an indication in inoperable or persistent/recurrent CTEPH but is not curative. Anticoagulation alone is insufficient. Epoprostenol and lung transplantation are reserved for inoperable or end-stage cases. CTEPH should be suspected in any patient with unexplained PH and a prior history of PE.
questions.topic: CTEPH pulmonary-endarterectomy riociguat chronic-thromboembolic-PH surgical-treatment

questions.question: A patient with acute RVF and elevated PVR is mechanically ventilated. The team is reviewing the ventilator settings and drug chart to avoid iatrogenic increases in PVR. Which of the following does NOT represent an iatrogenic cause of increased PVR?
options: A: Insufficient PEEP resulting in atelectasis
B: Permissive hypercapnia (PaCO2 8.5 kPa) to achieve lung-protective tidal volumes
C: Uncorrected metabolic acidaemia (pH 7.18)
D: High tidal volume causing alveolar overdistension
E: Prone positioning to improve oxygenation and V/Q matching
questions.explanation: Prone positioning improves oxygenation by recruiting dorsal lung units, reducing hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and V/Q mismatch — it REDUCES rather than increases PVR and improves both RV function and survival in severe ARDS. All other options increase PVR: hypercapnia and acidaemia are direct pulmonary vasoconstrictors; alveolar overdistension compresses alveolar vessels; and atelectasis collapses extra-alveolar vessels. The tension between lung-protective permissive hypercapnia and its own PVR-raising effect is a genuine clinical dilemma in ARDS with cor pulmonale.
questions.topic: PVR iatrogenic prone-positioning hypercapnia atelectasis ventilation RVF

questions.question: A patient with refractory acute RVF is being considered for short-term mechanical circulatory support. The intensivist reviews the haemodynamic effects of available devices. Which of the following best distinguishes the haemodynamic effect of VA-ECMO from that of the ImpellaRP or TandemHeart RVAD?
options: A: VA-ECMO reduces native RV stroke volume; percutaneous RVADs do not affect native RV stroke volume
B: Both VA-ECMO and percutaneous RVADs produce broadly similar haemodynamic changes, differing only in their access route
C: VA-ECMO increases PA systolic pressure; ImpellaRP and TandemHeart reduce PA systolic pressure
D: VA-ECMO reduces RA pressure only; ImpellaRP and TandemHeart also increase PA systolic pressure while reducing RA pressure
E: ImpellaRP and TandemHeart bypass the pulmonary circulation entirely and therefore unload the LV, unlike VA-ECMO
questions.explanation: Per Kapur et al. (2017) haemodynamic modelling: ImpellaRP and TandemHeart RVAD pump blood from the RA/RV to the PA — this reduces native RV stroke volume, INCREASES RV and PA peak systolic pressure, narrows PA pulse pressure, and decreases RA pressure. VA-ECMO drains the RA and returns blood to the systemic circulation (femoral artery), reducing RA pressure but having NO effect on PA systolic pressure or native RV stroke volume. This distinction is clinically important: VA-ECMO supports systemic perfusion but does not unload the pulmonary circulation, while percutaneous RVADs directly drive blood through the pulmonary vasculature.
questions.topic: ImpellaRP TandemHeart VA-ECMO RVAD haemodynamics MCS RA-pressure PA-pressure

questions.question: A patient with severe ischaemic cardiomyopathy undergoes LVAD implantation. Despite improvement in left-sided filling, the patient develops acute RVF on day 2 post-operatively. Which mechanism best explains this complication?
options: A: The LVAD directly increases pulmonary vascular resistance through mechanical effects of the impeller
B: LVAD-induced systemic inflammatory response selectively damages RV myocardium
C: LVAD unloading alters RV geometry via septal shift while simultaneously increasing RV preload demand as LV output rises
D: LVAD prevents the neurohormonal compensatory mechanisms that normally sustain a failing RV
E: LVAD implantation causes a paradoxical reduction in LV output leading to biventricular failure
questions.explanation: Two mechanisms drive post-LVAD RVF: (1) unloading the LV shifts the interventricular septum, directly distorting RV geometry and impairing function; (2) compared to the pre-operative low-output state, the LVAD now generates substantially increased LV output, obligating the RV to match this flow — effectively a major acute increase in RV preload. The RV, which may already have subclinical dysfunction, cannot compensate. Adequacy of RV function is the major determinant of outcome after LVAD insertion. A selective immune-mediated injury to RV myocardium is a superficially plausible-sounding distractor but is not an established mechanism of post-LVAD RVF.
questions.topic: LVAD post-operative-RVF ventricular-interdependence preload septal-shift mechanism

questions.question: Regarding the normal anatomy and physiology of the right ventricle:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Normal RV systolic pressure is 15–30 mmHg with pulmonary vascular resistance approximately one-half of systemic vascular resistance

statements.label: B
statements.text: The RV is described as 'tolerant' of preload but 'intolerant' of acute increases in afterload due to its thin wall and lower muscle mass

statements.label: C
statements.text: In health, myocardial perfusion of the RV occurs during systole only, unlike the LV which is perfused only during diastole

statements.label: D
statements.text: The RV free wall thickness is 2–5 mm, with approximately one-sixth the muscle mass of the LV

questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RV-anatomy physiology perfusion preload afterload wall-thickness PVR
questions.explanation: Citing normal RV systolic pressure as 15–30 mmHg with pulmonary vascular resistance approximately one-half of systemic vascular resistance is false — the RV systolic pressure figure is accurate, but PVR is actually closer to one-fifth of SVR (PVR <250 dynes·sec/cm⁻⁵ vs SVR 800–1200). Describing the RV as tolerant of preload but intolerant of acute increases in afterload, due to its thin wall and lower muscle mass, is true — the RV compensates well for volume overload but haemodynamically collapses rapidly with acute afterload increases such as massive PE, unlike the LV, which can hypertrophy to sustain chronic pressure overload. Claiming that in health the RV is perfused during systole only, unlike the LV which is perfused only during diastole, is false — the RV is actually perfused in BOTH systole AND diastole; this bi-phasic perfusion can be lost in pulmonary hypertension when high RV systolic pressure exceeds coronary perfusion pressure. Citing RV free wall thickness of 2–5 mm, with approximately one-sixth the muscle mass of the LV, is true (LV wall 8–10 mm).

questions.question: Regarding RV dysfunction in acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Increasing PEEP uniformly reduces RV afterload by recruiting previously collapsed alveolar vessels

statements.label: B
statements.text: PaO2/FiO2 ratio <150 mmHg is among the predictors of RV dysfunction in patients with ARDS

statements.label: C
statements.text: RV dysfunction occurs in less than 10% of patients with ARDS and is not independently associated with ICU mortality

statements.label: D
statements.text: Prone positioning improves both RV function and survival in severe ARDS

questions.topic: ARDS RVD cor-pulmonale PEEP prone-positioning PaO2-FiO2 predictors
questions.explanation: Claiming increasing PEEP uniformly reduces RV afterload by recruiting collapsed alveolar vessels is false — PEEP raises RA pressure throughout the ventilatory cycle (reducing RV preload) and at higher levels compresses alveolar vessels, increasing RV afterload; its net effect on RV dysfunction is often detrimental rather than uniformly beneficial. A PaO2/FiO2 ratio under 150 mmHg being among the predictors of RV dysfunction in ARDS is true, alongside lower respiratory tract infection as the ARDS cause, driving pressure ≥18 cmH2O, and PaCO2 ≥48 mmHg. Claiming RV dysfunction occurs in under 10% of ARDS patients and is not independently associated with ICU mortality is false — it actually occurs in 22–55% of ARDS patients and is independently associated with increased mortality. Prone positioning improving both RV function and survival in severe ARDS is true — it recruits dorsal lung, improves oxygenation, and reduces hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction.

questions.question: Regarding the classification and definition of pulmonary hypertension (PH):

statements.label: A
statements.text: A high pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (>25 mmHg) distinguishes post-capillary PH from pre-capillary PH

statements.label: B
statements.text: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and other chronic lung diseases (WHO Group III) are the most common cause of pulmonary hypertension overall

statements.label: C
statements.text: PH is defined as mean pulmonary artery pressure ≥25 mmHg at rest, measured at right heart catheterisation

statements.label: D
statements.text: Pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease is classified as WHO Group I (pulmonary arterial hypertension)

questions.topic: pulmonary-hypertension WHO-classification PAH Group-I Group-II PCWP post-capillary
questions.explanation: Citing a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure above 25 mmHg as the threshold distinguishing post-capillary from pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension is false — the accepted threshold is actually PCWP >15 mmHg. Naming COPD and other chronic lung diseases (WHO Group III) as the most common overall cause of pulmonary hypertension is false — left ventricular failure (WHO Group II) is actually the commonest cause in the general population. Defining pulmonary hypertension as mean pulmonary artery pressure ≥25 mmHg at rest on right heart catheterisation is true — this remains the diagnostic gold standard. Classifying pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease as WHO Group I is false — it is actually Group II (post-capillary PH); Group I is idiopathic/heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension and associated conditions, and confusing the two groups matters clinically since Group I-specific therapies can be harmful in Group II disease.

questions.question: Regarding pharmacological management of acute RVF:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Dobutamine (β1-agonist) and milrinone (PDE-3 inhibitor) are both inodilators that reduce SVR and PVR, but milrinone has a substantially longer half-life (~2.5 hours)

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sildenafil is preferred over inhaled nitric oxide for haemodynamic rescue in acute decompensated RVF due to its faster onset of action

statements.label: C
statements.text: Systemic hypotension in acute RVF reduces right coronary artery perfusion pressure, worsening myocardial ischaemia and RV function in a downward spiral

statements.label: D
statements.text: A relatively low heart rate is preferred in acute RVF to allow adequate diastolic filling time and maximise stroke volume, similar to the management of severe aortic stenosis

questions.topic: RVF-management milrinone dobutamine sildenafil inhaled-NO heart-rate coronary-perfusion
questions.explanation: Describing dobutamine and milrinone as both inodilators that reduce SVR and PVR, with milrinone having a substantially longer half-life of around 2.5 hours, is true — that longer half-life is why a milrinone loading dose is usually avoided, since resulting hypotension is harder to reverse. Preferring sildenafil over inhaled nitric oxide for haemodynamic rescue in acute decompensated RVF due to faster onset is false — sildenafil has a long terminal half-life of 4–18 hours with significant hypotension risk and is used in stable chronic disease, whereas inhaled NO's fast onset, short half-life and selective pulmonary action make it the preferred acute agent. Systemic hypotension in acute RVF reducing right coronary perfusion pressure and worsening myocardial ischaemia in a downward spiral is true — this is the RV ischaemic spiral. Preferring a relatively low heart rate in acute RVF to allow diastolic filling, similar to severe aortic stenosis, is false — a relatively HIGH heart rate is actually preferred, to limit distension and maintain output when stroke volume is limited, the opposite of the AS strategy.

questions.question: Regarding management of pulmonary embolism with right ventricular compromise:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) occurs in approximately 15–20% of patients following acute PE due to incomplete clot resolution

statements.label: B
statements.text: In intermediate-high risk PE (haemodynamically stable with echocardiographic RV strain), systemic thrombolysis is proven to reduce 30-day mortality

statements.label: C
statements.text: Systemic thrombolysis is the primary reperfusion strategy in massive PE when there are no absolute contraindications

statements.label: D
statements.text: Massive (high-risk) PE is defined by haemodynamic compromise with BP drop >40 mmHg sustained for >15 minutes in the absence of another identifiable cause

questions.topic: PE thrombolysis massive-PE intermediate-risk CTEPH mortality PEITHO haemodynamic-compromise
questions.explanation: Citing chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension as occurring in 15–20% of patients after acute PE, due to incomplete clot resolution, is false — the true rate is 2–4%; pulmonary endarterectomy is potentially curative for proximal disease. Claiming systemic thrombolysis in intermediate-high risk PE (haemodynamically stable with echo RV strain) is proven to reduce 30-day mortality is false — the PEITHO trial showed it prevents further haemodynamic deterioration but does NOT reduce mortality, while significantly increasing major (including intracranial) haemorrhage. Systemic thrombolysis being the primary reperfusion strategy in massive PE without absolute contraindications is true. Defining massive (high-risk) PE by a sustained BP drop of more than 40 mmHg for over 15 minutes without another identifiable cause is also true — this is the haemodynamic-compromise threshold requiring primary reperfusion.

questions.question: Regarding heart–lung interactions and positive pressure ventilation in the context of RV failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Positive intrathoracic pressure during mechanical inspiration increases right atrial pressure, reducing the venous pressure gradient and decreasing RV preload

statements.label: B
statements.text: Adding PEEP attenuates the reduction in RV preload compared with zero-PEEP positive pressure ventilation

statements.label: C
statements.text: In a mechanically ventilated patient with echocardiographic RV failure, an elevated pulse pressure variation (PPV) reflects RV afterload dependency rather than fluid responsiveness

statements.label: D
statements.text: At atelectatic lung volumes, alveolar vessel resistance increases due to loss of radial tethering, while extra-alveolar vessels are unaffected

questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: IPPV PEEP RV-preload PPV afterload-dependency FRC extra-alveolar-vessels heart-lung
questions.explanation: Positive intrathoracic pressure during mechanical inspiration increasing right atrial pressure, reducing the venous return gradient and decreasing RV preload is true. Claiming PEEP attenuates the reduction in RV preload compared with zero-PEEP positive pressure ventilation is false — PEEP augments RA pressure throughout the WHOLE ventilatory cycle, not just inspiration, so it increases rather than attenuates the preload-reducing effect. An elevated pulse pressure variation in a ventilated patient with echo-confirmed RV failure reflecting afterload dependency rather than fluid responsiveness is true — misinterpreting it as hypovolaemia and giving fluids can severely worsen RV dilatation and LV diastolic impairment. Claiming that at atelectatic lung volumes alveolar vessel resistance rises from loss of radial tethering while extra-alveolar vessels are unaffected is false — it is the EXTRA-alveolar vessels that lose radial tethering and collapse at low lung volumes; alveolar vessel resistance instead rises separately with lung OVER-inflation via direct compression, together producing the U-shaped PVR–lung-volume curve with its minimum at FRC.

questions.question: Regarding short-term mechanical circulatory support devices for acute RVF:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The ImpellaRP reduces RA pressure and increases RV and PA peak systolic pressure by pumping blood from the right atrium to the pulmonary artery

statements.label: B
statements.text: VA-ECMO reduces RA pressure but has no significant effect on PA systolic pressure or native RV stroke volume

statements.label: C
statements.text: VA-ECMO directly unloads the pulmonary circulation and therefore reduces pulmonary artery pressure, making it the preferred device to reduce RV afterload

statements.label: D
statements.text: The surgically implanted CentriMag RVAD is CE-marked for circulatory support for up to 30 days and allows patient mobilisation as femoral access is not required

questions.topic: ImpellaRP TandemHeart VA-ECMO CentriMag RVAD MCS RA-pressure PA-pressure haemodynamics
questions.explanation: The ImpellaRP reducing RA pressure and increasing RV and PA peak systolic pressure, by pumping blood from the right atrium to the pulmonary artery, is true — this narrows the PA pulse pressure. VA-ECMO reducing RA pressure but having no significant effect on PA systolic pressure or native RV stroke volume is true — it drains the RA and returns blood to the systemic (femoral) artery, bypassing rather than supporting the pulmonary circulation. Claiming VA-ECMO directly unloads the pulmonary circulation and is therefore the preferred device to reduce RV afterload is false — VA-ECMO does NOT reduce pulmonary artery pressure; it supports systemic perfusion instead, and pulmonary vasodilators such as inhaled NO are needed concurrently to directly reduce RV afterload. The surgically implanted CentriMag RVAD being CE-marked for up to 30 days and allowing mobilisation, since femoral access is not required, is true — central cannulation distinguishes it from percutaneous femoral devices.

questions.question: Regarding preload management and ventricular interdependence in acute RVF:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In RVF with elevated pulmonary vascular resistance (e.g. PAH), aggressive fluid loading improves RV output by exploiting residual Frank-Starling reserve

statements.label: B
statements.text: Excessive RV dilatation constrained by the pericardium shifts the interventricular septum leftward, impairing LV diastolic filling and further reducing cardiac output

statements.label: C
statements.text: Fluid responsiveness in acute RVF should be assessed using large volume boluses (500–1000 ml), since smaller volumes provide unreliable information

statements.label: D
statements.text: In RV myocardial infarction, aggressive diuresis is the preferred initial strategy to offload the pressure-overloaded ventricle

questions.topic: preload CVP RVEDP PAH ventricular-interdependence fluid-loading pericardium RVMI
questions.explanation: Claiming aggressive fluid loading improves RV output in RVF with elevated pulmonary vascular resistance (e.g. PAH) by exploiting residual Frank-Starling reserve is false — the RV is already dilated in this setting, so further loading worsens dilatation, increases tricuspid regurgitation, and shifts the septum leftward, impairing LV filling and REDUCING cardiac output; diuresis or ultrafiltration is often warranted instead. Excessive RV dilatation, constrained by the pericardium, shifting the interventricular septum leftward and impairing LV diastolic filling is true — this ventricular interdependence is a major mechanism of LV diastolic failure in advanced RVF. Recommending large-volume boluses (500–1000 ml) to assess fluid responsiveness in acute RVF is false — small boluses (50–100 ml) with close CVP monitoring are recommended specifically to detect early over-distension, which large boluses risk causing outright. Recommending aggressive diuresis as the preferred initial strategy in RV myocardial infarction is false — afterload is normal in RVMI, so RVEDP often needs to be maintained HIGHER via adequate filling (not diuresis) to sustain output through the Frank-Starling mechanism; diuresis is instead the correct strategy in the opposite scenario — the dilated, afterload-overloaded RV.

questions.question: Regarding echocardiographic assessment of the right ventricle in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is the recommended first-line modality for RV size, function and load assessment in the ICU

statements.label: B
statements.text: TOE should replace TTE as the primary RV assessment tool in all mechanically ventilated ICU patients due to superior image quality

statements.label: C
statements.text: Use of TOE in haemodynamically unstable ICU patients with unexplained haemodynamic disturbance is a Category I indication when other tests are inconclusive or the patient is too unstable for other investigations

statements.label: D
statements.text: The risk of oesophageal perforation from TOE is approximately 1 in 50 procedures, with an overall mortality of approximately 1 in 10,000

questions.topic: TTE TOE echocardiography RV-assessment Category-I oesophageal-perforation ICU
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography being the recommended first-line modality for RV size, function and load assessment in the ICU is true, per ESICM guidelines — the RV's complex shape and retrosternal position make TTE challenging, but it remains the initial approach. Claiming TOE should replace TTE as the primary RV assessment tool in all mechanically ventilated patients, due to superior image quality, is false — TTE remains first-line, with TOE reserved for cases where TTE is technically inadequate (high PEEP, intrathoracic surgery, chest trauma); routine TOE in every ventilated patient is neither indicated nor risk-free. Using TOE in haemodynamically unstable ICU patients with unexplained haemodynamic disturbance as a Category I indication, when other tests are inconclusive or the patient is too unstable for alternatives, is true. Citing the oesophageal perforation risk from TOE as approximately 1 in 50 procedures is false — the actual risk is approximately 1 in 500, a materially lower and different figure; overall mortality is approximately 1 in 10,000.

questions.question: Regarding right ventricular failure in the context of advanced cardiac therapy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Primary graft dysfunction of the right ventricle (PGD-RV) is diagnosed by high right-sided filling, HIGH left-sided filling and HIGH cardiac output — OR need for RV mechanical circulatory support within 24 hours of surgery

statements.label: B
statements.text: Following lung transplantation for PAH, LV failure may develop because the LV — chronically exposed to reduced preload — is suddenly exposed to a dramatically increased cardiac output once pulmonary vascular resistance normalises

statements.label: C
statements.text: Heart transplantation alone is contraindicated in end-stage right heart failure caused by irreversible pulmonary hypertensive disease

statements.label: D
statements.text: Long-term LVAD devices repositioned to provide right-sided support are currently recommended as the standard of care for isolated refractory RVF

questions.topic: PGD-RV transplantation PAH lung-transplant LVAD TAH biventricular-support end-stage
questions.explanation: Diagnosing primary graft dysfunction of the right ventricle by high right-sided filling together with HIGH left-sided filling and HIGH cardiac output, or the need for RV mechanical support within 24 hours of surgery, is false — the actual criteria pair high right-sided filling with LOW left-sided filling and LOW cardiac output (with low pulmonary pressures), not high left-sided values. LV failure developing after lung transplantation for PAH, because a chronically underfilled LV is suddenly exposed to a dramatically increased cardiac output once pulmonary vascular resistance normalises, is true — this flooding of an unprepared LV is a recognised cause of early post-transplant mortality. Heart transplantation alone being contraindicated in end-stage right heart failure caused by irreversible pulmonary hypertensive disease is true — the donor heart's normally-adapted RV cannot tolerate the recipient's elevated afterload. Recommending long-term LVAD devices repositioned for right-sided support as the standard of care for isolated refractory RVF is false — this approach is described only in small case series with significant complications and is not currently standard care; the Total Artificial Heart is the established biventricular option instead.

questions.question: The figure shows haemodynamic data from a canine model of progressive pulmonary artery banding (PAB), with a systemic vasoconstrictor (phenylephrine) subsequently administered at maximal banding. As MAP and right coronary artery (RCA) pressure are restored towards baseline with phenylephrine, despite no change in the degree of mechanical pulmonary outflow obstruction, what happens to RV end-diastolic pressure (RVEDP) and cardiac output (CO)?
options: A: Both RVEDP and CO show no significant change, since coronary perfusion is not the limiting factor in RV pressure overload
B: RVEDP falls but CO also falls further, reflecting a net negative inotropic effect of phenylephrine
C: RVEDP rises further and CO continues to fall, since afterload remains unchanged
D: RVEDP falls and CO increases, as improved right coronary perfusion pressure restores RV contractility despite unchanged mechanical obstruction
E: RVEDP and CO both rise, since phenylephrine directly increases pulmonary blood flow
questions.explanation: In this canine PAB model, progressive mechanical pulmonary outflow obstruction caused rising RVEDP and falling CO as RV systolic pressure approached and then exceeded right coronary artery perfusion pressure, compromising RV perfusion. Administering phenylephrine, without altering the mechanical obstruction, raised MAP and RCA pressure, restoring right coronary perfusion pressure and RV contractility — RVEDP fell and CO rose. This illustrates why maintaining systemic (and hence coronary perfusion) pressure is a core physiological target in RV pressure overload, independent of any change in the mechanical afterload itself.
questions.topic: coronary perfusion RCA pressure PAB phenylephrine RVEDP cardiac output

questions.question: A patient with severe RV dysfunction and known severe tricuspid regurgitation has CVP monitoring in place. Panel B of the figure shows the CVP waveform pattern most likely to be seen in this patient, in contrast to the normal trace in Panel A. Which best describes the abnormality shown in Panel B and its underlying mechanism?
options: A: An accentuated a wave with an attenuated y descent, reflecting reduced RV compliance
B: A prominent x descent with an absent c wave, consistent with constrictive pericarditis
C: Fused c-v waves with a sharp y descent, from retrograde filling via the incompetent tricuspid valve
D: An isolated absent a wave, indicating loss of atrial contraction in complete heart block
E: Loss of the x descent with preserved a and v waves, indicating atrial fibrillation
questions.explanation: Panel B shows the CVP pattern of severe tricuspid regurgitation: the incompetent valve allows retrograde systolic filling of the right atrium, producing a dominant v wave that fuses with the preceding c wave, followed by a sharp y descent as the elevated right atrial volume empties rapidly once the tricuspid valve opens. This contrasts with Panel C (reduced RV compliance, e.g. RV infarction or constrictive physiology), which shows an accentuated a wave and attenuated y wave instead.
questions.topic: CVP waveform tricuspid regurgitation fused c v wave y descent JVP

questions.question: The figure shows 28-day survival in ARDS patients stratified by presence or absence of cor pulmonale (defined echocardiographically as a dilated RV with septal dyskinesia). Which best describes the finding and its statistical significance?
options: A: Cor pulmonale is associated with significantly worse 28-day survival (p<0.01), with the survival curves separating within the first week
B: The survival difference is statistically significant only beyond day 20, with no separation of the curves before this point
C: There is no meaningful difference in survival between the two groups, confirming cor pulmonale is not prognostically relevant in ARDS
D: Cor pulmonale is associated with worse survival, but the difference disappears by day 28 as both groups converge
E: Cor pulmonale shows a trend towards better survival that does not reach statistical significance
questions.explanation: The Boissier et al. survival curves show early and sustained separation between ARDS patients with vs without echocardiographic cor pulmonale, with significantly worse 28-day survival in the cor pulmonale group (log-rank p<0.01). The gap is apparent from the first week and persists to day 28 without convergence, consistent with RV dysfunction being an independent predictor of mortality in ARDS rather than a late or transient phenomenon.
questions.topic: cor pulmonale ARDS survival Kaplan-Meier mortality prognosis Boissier

questions.question: The figure compares normal left ventricular (red) and right ventricular (blue) pressure-volume loops. Which feature of the RV loop, evident in the figure, best explains why the point of end-systole is harder to define in the RV compared to the LV?
options: A: The RV loop shows a much larger enclosed area than the LV loop, reflecting higher RV stroke work
B: The RV loop demonstrates a higher peak systolic pressure than the LV loop, obscuring the transition to diastole
C: The RV loop shows a distinctly rectangular shape with sharp corners identical to the LV, making end-systole equally easy to identify in both ventricles
D: The RV loop shows a prolonged isovolumetric contraction phase compared to the LV, blurring the onset of ejection
E: The RV loop is more triangular, with a shortened or absent isovolumetric relaxation phase as ejection continues despite falling RV pressure
questions.explanation: The RV loop appears triangular rather than rectangular because ejection continues despite falling RV pressure — momentum carries blood forward in the low-pressure, low-resistance pulmonary circulation even as ventricular pressure declines — shortening or abolishing the isovolumetric relaxation phase and blurring the transition from systole to diastole. The LV, ejecting into a high-resistance systemic circulation, shows well-defined isovolumetric contraction and relaxation phases producing sharp 'corners' on its loop.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RV LV pressure-volume loop shape triangular isovolumetric relaxation momentum

questions.question: The figure compares the effect of acutely rising afterload (vessel pressure) on stroke volume for the RV (blue) and LV (red). Based on the pattern shown, which best explains the clinical relevance of this difference in a patient with acute massive pulmonary embolism?
options: A: The RV's stroke volume is unaffected by afterload within the physiological range shown, and only fails once extreme, supra-systemic pressures are reached
B: The curve demonstrates that chronic, not acute, increases in afterload are poorly tolerated by the RV, while acute increases are well compensated
C: Because the RV tolerates acute afterload increases as well as the LV, haemodynamic collapse in massive PE is primarily due to hypoxaemia rather than mechanical RV failure
D: The RV and LV both show a steep, near-identical fall in stroke volume with rising afterload, reflecting shared intolerance of afterload

E: The RV's stroke volume falls steeply with acute increases in afterload, unlike the LV, explaining why acute massive PE can rapidly cause haemodynamic collapse despite the RV's tolerance of volume overload
questions.explanation: The figure shows RV stroke volume falling steeply as afterload (vessel pressure) rises acutely, in contrast to the LV which maintains stroke volume across a much wider range of pressures — reflecting the RV's thin wall and lower muscle mass. In acute massive PE, mechanical obstruction acutely raises RV afterload; because the RV cannot compensate as rapidly or effectively as the LV can, stroke volume and cardiac output fall precipitously, producing prompt haemodynamic collapse. This contrasts with the RV's much better tolerance of gradual, chronic afterload rises (e.g. in pulmonary hypertension), where hypertrophy allows adaptation over time.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RV afterload intolerance stroke volume pulmonary embolism acute chronic adaptation

questions.question: A ventilated patient develops acute hypotension. A parasternal short-axis transthoracic echocardiographic view is obtained (see figure). Based on the appearance shown, which underlying process is most likely?
options: A: Acute RV pressure or volume overload causing septal flattening and a 'D-shaped' LV cross-section due to ventricular interdependence
B: Normal biventricular geometry with a preserved circular LV cross-section, making RV pathology unlikely
C: Pericardial tamponade with a circumferential pericardial effusion compressing all chambers equally
D: Isolated severe LV systolic dysfunction with a dilated, globular LV cavity
E: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy bulging into the LV cavity
questions.explanation: This parasternal short-axis view shows a dilated RV with a flattened interventricular septum, distorting the normally circular LV cross-section into a 'D' shape. This is the echocardiographic hallmark of ventricular interdependence: RV pressure or volume overload raises RV-EDP relative to LV-EDP, pushing the septum towards the LV during diastole, impairing LV filling and reducing the mechanical efficiency of LV contraction — even though the LV itself may be structurally and functionally normal.
questions.topic: echocardiography D-shaped septum ventricular interdependence parasternal short-axis RV overload

questions.question: According to European heart failure registry data, approximately what proportion of acute heart failure hospital admissions are attributable to acute right ventricular failure, and what is the associated in-hospital mortality range?
options: A: Approximately 50% of admissions; in-hospital mortality comparable to left-sided heart failure
B: Acute RVF is not separately captured in AHF registries, so its contribution cannot be estimated
C: 2–9% of admissions; in-hospital mortality 30–50%
D: 20–30% of admissions; in-hospital mortality 5–17%
E: 2–9% of admissions; in-hospital mortality 5–17%
questions.explanation: Acute RVF accounts for a relatively small proportion (2-9%) of AHF admissions in European registries (Harjola et al. 2016; Spinar et al. 2011), but carries an in-hospital mortality of 5-17% — and worsening RV function is independently associated with poorer ICU prognosis across a wide range of critical illnesses (ARDS, MI, PE, decompensated chronic lung disease, post-cardiac surgery). Early recognition is emphasised precisely because RVF, while a minority presentation, carries disproportionate risk.
questions.topic: RVF epidemiology prevalence in-hospital mortality registry AHF admissions
